experiment: verifier head-to-head v2 (re-feed vs true-KV replay) — cross-model, with CIs
thesis: Replay-based verifiers (DiFR 2511.20621, TOPLOC 2501.16007, SVIP, VeriLLM) reconstruct a token's signature by re-feeding the transcript; the commitment is the decode-time distribution. Re-feed (bulk prefill) != decode, so the verifier drifts at low-margin tokens. A thaw verifier replays from the true decode-time KV (.thawkv) and matches the commitment by construction (~0 error).

config.verifier: argmax membership + logit tolerance; low-margin = top1-top2 gap < 0.3 nats; rates carry Wilson 95% CIs

headline: Cross-family and consistent: at low-margin decision tokens, re-feed drift exceeds the top1-top2 gap 27.6% (Qwen, CI 24.5-30.9) / 22.8% (Llama, CI 20.6-25.3) of the time — ~1 in 4 swaps hide inside the verifier's own re-feed noise. True-KV (thaw receipt) verifier: 0.

A re-feed argmax-verifier would accept a runner-up forgery at 4.6% (Qwen, CI 3.3-6.4) / 3.3% (Llama, CI 2.5-4.5) of low-margin tokens, and wrongly reject genuine output at ~3.5-4.6%. True-KV verifier: 0 in both directions.
At a tight logit tolerance (tau=0.01 nats), re-feed verification false-rejects 8.6% (Qwen) / 11.5% (Llama) of genuine tokens; true-KV: 0%.

Conclusion: replay-from-re-feed verification has both a false-reject cost on genuine output and a forgery-acceptance hole at the tokens that matter, in two model families with tight CIs; replay-from-true-KV (thaw's .thawkv inference receipt) removes both.
scope: The true-KV arm is sound by construction (decode-time distribution = the commitment; .thawkv restores it bit-identically per thaw's separate receipts). This run measures the re-feed verifier against that ground truth on fresh greedy generations across two model families. A head-to-head against a deployed DiFR/TOPLOC codebase remains a further step.
